Title: Mitochondrial iron chelation ameliorates cigarette-smoke induced bronchitis and emphysema in mice

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Mixed 129S4/SvJae and C57BL/6 background; WT and Irp2-/- littermates
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Lung mitochondria, especially airway/alveolar epithelial cell mitochondria
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential cofactor for heme and Fe-S cluster biology; excess mitochondrial iron promotes oxidative injury and inflammation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cigarette smoke-induced mitochondrial iron loading, mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired mucociliary clearance, pulmonary inflammation, bronchitis and emphysema; loss of Irp2 is protective
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout mouse (Irp2-/-); RIP-Seq; lung microarray; siRNA/shRNA knockdown in human airway epithelial cells; metabolite/iron profiling; mitochondrial function assays; histology
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Irp2 expression and activity increase in lungs after cigarette smoke exposure and in human COPD-related samples/cells. 2. Irp2 deficiency protects mice from cigarette smoke-induced emphysema, airway remodeling, inflammation, and impaired mucociliary clearance. 3. IRP2 regulates mitochondrial pathways, increases mitochondrial iron loading and cytochrome c oxidase activity, driving mitochondrial dysfunction in experimental COPD.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Iron-responsive element-binding protein; RNA-binding iron homeostasis regulator
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Increased in lung epithelial cells and alveolar macrophages after cigarette smoke exposure
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Localized to type I and type II alveolar cells, secretory airway cells, ciliated airway cells, and alveolar macrophages; induced by cigarette smoke/CSE
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cigarette smoke exposure; cigarette smoke extract; deferoxamine for IRP2 stabilization in RIP-Seq
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RIP-Seq: GSE57073; Mouse lung microarray: GSE57048; human comparison datasets discussed: LGRC GSE47460; ECLIPSE GSE54837
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Primary experimentally validated focal gene of the study; acts post-transcriptionally by binding IRE-containing transcripts; paper supports a mitochondrial iron axis in COPD pathogenesis
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression previously associated with embryonic lethality; elevated IRP2 is deleterious to lung mitochondrial homeostasis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential therapeutic target for reducing pathological mitochondrial iron loading; inhibition of IRP2 or its downstream mitochondrial iron axis may ameliorate disease

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Mitochondrial electron transport chain / cytochrome c oxidase assembly
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Synthesis of cytochrome c oxidase 2 protein
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Functional cytochrome c oxidase complex IV assembly
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Assembly factor for terminal respiratory complex
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for respiratory complex IV activity that in turn influences mitochondrial iron loading
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for COX activity in this study context
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced Sco2 function lowers COX activity and protects mice from cigarette smoke-induced mucociliary clearance defects, pulmonary inflammation, and mitochondrial non-heme iron loading
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic mouse models (Sco2ki/ko, Sco2ki/ki); mucociliary clearance assay; BAL inflammation/protein measurements; mitochondrial iron quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SCO2-deficient mice with impaired COX assembly/activity are protected from cigarette smoke-induced bronchitis phenotypes. 2. Reduced SCO2 function lowers pulmonary inflammatory markers and BAL protein leakage after smoke exposure. 3. SCO2 deficiency prevents smoke-associated rise in mitochondrial non-heme iron, linking COX to mitochondrial iron regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Previously reported to be higher in lungs of COPD subjects; functional testing performed in mutant mice
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Homozygous deletion is embryonic lethal; stronger COX deficiency may impair respiration
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports targeting COX assembly/activity to modulate mitochondrial iron overload and COPD-like injury
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as mechanistic validation rather than as the primary discovery gene in the paper

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Mitochondrial iron-sulfur cluster / iron homeostasis pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Controls mitochondrial iron homeostasis affecting iron overload status
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced frataxin expression increases mitochondrial non-heme and heme iron and worsens baseline mucociliary clearance and lung IL-6 inflammation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic mouse model (Fxnki/ko); mitochondrial iron quantification; mucociliary clearance assay; IL-6 measurement
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Frataxin-deficient mice show increased mitochondrial iron loading. 2. Elevated mitochondrial iron is associated with impaired baseline mucociliary clearance. 3. Frataxin deficiency increases baseline pulmonary IL-6, supporting pathogenicity of mitochondrial iron overload.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Lower frataxin associated with cigarette smoke in WT lungs and CSE-treated human airway epithelial cells
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Reduced by cigarette smoke/CSE; mutant mice used to model increased mitochondrial iron loading
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cigarette smoke exposure; cigarette smoke extract
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Frataxin insufficiency is deleterious and linked to mitochondrial dysfunction
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining frataxin function may prevent mitochondrial iron overload-related pathology
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as mechanistic support that mitochondrial iron overload is pathogenic, not as the main discovery target
